agent_output:
# Domain Analysis — Conflicting Stakeholder Requirements: Courier Real-Time Location

---

## ESCALATION FIRST

This is a **conflicting requirements** situation. Both teams present their position as non-negotiable, but both positions as stated **cannot coexist**. Before any implementation begins, this conflict must be resolved at the business level. My analysis below provides the domain-grounded framework for that resolution.

---

```yaml
domain_analysis:
  business_context: |
    Real-time courier tracking during last-mile delivery. The core business
    process is: a customer has placed an order, a courier is en route, and the
    customer wants confidence that their delivery is progressing. This is a
    standard "order fulfillment visibility" domain problem present across all
    major logistics platforms.
  ubiquitous_language:
    - term: Courier
      definition: >
        The human driver/rider performing the physical delivery. A person with
        privacy rights, safety concerns, and agency over their working conditions.
        NOT a GPS beacon. NOT a package.
    - term: Delivery
      definition: >
        The fulfillment event spanning from dispatch to proof-of-delivery.
        Has a lifecycle: assigned → picked up → in transit → arriving → delivered.
    - term: ETA
      definition: >
        Estimated Time of Arrival — the primary information artifact the customer
        actually needs to plan around. Derived from location, traffic, route, and
        historical data. More useful than raw coordinates.
    - term: Tracking
      definition: >
        Customer-facing visibility into delivery progress. CRITICAL DISTINCTION:
        tracking the DELIVERY (business event) vs. tracking the COURIER (human).
        These are fundamentally different domain concepts being conflated.
    - term: Location Precision
      definition: >
        The granularity of positional data exposed. Ranges from exact GPS
        coordinates (sub-meter) to zone-based approximation ("3 stops away",
        "in your neighborhood") to pure status-based ("in transit").
    - term: Delivery Zone
      definition: >
        An approximate geographic area indicating courier proximity without
        exposing exact position. Standard industry pattern (e.g., "your courier
        is in your area").
  bounded_context: >
    Order Fulfillment / Last-Mile Delivery Visibility. This sits at the
    intersection of Customer Experience and Courier Operations — two bounded
    contexts with legitimately different invariants.
```

---

## The Core Domain Truth

**Both teams are wrong about what they're asking for, and both are right about what they need.**

- **Customer Success** says "exact GPS coordinates at all times." But the actual business need is **delivery certainty** — the customer wants to know: *Is it coming? When will it arrive? Is there a problem?* No customer wakes up wanting a latitude/longitude pair. They want confidence.

- **Operations** says "location must never be shown." But the actual business need is **courier dignity and retention** — drivers don't want to feel surveilled, micro-managed, or stalked. They are not opposed to customers knowing a delivery is 5 minutes away.

The conflict exists because both teams have **encoded a solution** ("show exact GPS" / "show nothing") instead of articulating the **business need** ("delivery confidence" / "courier privacy and retention").

---

```yaml
business_rules:
  - rule: >
      Customers must have continuous visibility into delivery progress from
      dispatch through proof-of-delivery.
    invariant: true
    testable_assertion: >
      At any point during an active delivery, a customer can open the app
      and determine whether their delivery is progressing and approximately
      when it will arrive.

  - rule: >
      Courier exact location (precise GPS coordinates) must NEVER be exposed
      directly to customers.
    invariant: true
    testable_assertion: >
      No API response or UI element displays courier latitude/longitude with
      precision greater than ~500m radius. No coordinate pair is ever present
      in customer-facing payloads.

  - rule: >
      Courier location data at full precision is an INTERNAL operational asset,
      not a customer-facing data point.
    invariant: true
    testable_assertion: >
      Full-precision courier GPS is only accessible to dispatch, operations,
      and safety systems. Customer-facing services receive only derived/degraded
      location data.

  - rule: >
      Customer-facing tracking must provide an ETA that updates in real-time
      based on courier progress.
    invariant: true
    testable_assertion: >
      ETA updates at least every 60 seconds during active delivery and reflects
      actual courier movement and traffic conditions.

  - rule: >
      Customer-facing tracking may show approximate courier location as a
      zone or region, not a point.
    invariant: false
    testable_assertion: >
      If a map is shown, courier position is represented as an area
      (circle, zone, "nearby" indicator) rather than a precise pin.
      The approximation zone is no smaller than 200-500 meters.

  - rule: >
      Courier must be able to pause location sharing during breaks or
      non-delivery periods without operational penalty.
    invariant: true
    testable_assertion: >
      When a courier has no active delivery, no location data (even
      approximate) is shared with any customer. Location sharing is strictly
      scoped to active delivery lifecycle.

  - rule: >
      Delivery status transitions must be exposed to the customer as
      discrete, meaningful business events.
    invariant: true
    testable_assertion: >
      Customer receives notifications at: courier assigned, picked up,
      in transit, arriving (< X min), delivered. Each is a business event,
      not a GPS reading.
```

---

```yaml
acceptance_criteria:
  - criterion: Customer can track active delivery progress
    given: A delivery is in "in transit" status assigned to a courier
    when: The customer opens the tracking view
    then: >
      They see: (1) current delivery status, (2) a real-time ETA,
      (3) an approximate location indicator (zone-based, not point-based),
      (4) status history of the delivery lifecycle.

  - criterion: Courier exact position is never leaked to customer
    given: A courier is actively delivering an order
    when: Any customer-facing API endpoint is called
    then: >
      The response contains NO field with GPS precision finer than a
      ~500m radius. Coordinates, if present, are quantized/jittered.
      No customer-side reverse-engineering of exact position is feasible.

  - criterion: ETA is the primary tracking artifact
    given: A delivery is in transit
    when: The customer views tracking
    then: >
      The ETA is the most prominent element. It updates dynamically.
      The map/zone indicator is supplementary, not primary.

  - criterion: Location sharing is delivery-scoped
    given: A courier has completed their delivery (status = delivered)
    when: The customer attempts to view courier location
    then: >
      No location data is available. The tracking view shows completed
      status and proof-of-delivery only.

  - criterion: Courier on break is invisible
    given: A courier is between deliveries or on break
    when: Any customer (even one with a pending future order) queries
    then: No location data of any kind is returned.

  edge_cases:
    - scenario: Courier is stationary for extended period (traffic, breakdown)
      expected_behavior: >
        ETA adjusts upward. Status may change to "delayed." Customer
        receives a proactive notification. Approximate zone does NOT
        update to reveal exact stall location (could be courier's home
        between batched deliveries).

    - scenario: Customer screenshots/records approximate location over time to triangulate
      expected_behavior: >
        Zone approximation must include sufficient jitter/randomization
        that sequential observations cannot reconstruct an exact route.
        Update frequency of zone indicator should be throttled (every
        60-90 seconds, not real-time).

    - scenario: Multiple deliveries by same courier — customer sees pattern
      expected_behavior: >
        Each delivery is an independent tracking session. No historical
        location data persists in customer-facing systems. Customer cannot
        correlate courier movement across deliveries.

    - scenario: Courier approaches customer address — precision matters most
      expected_behavior: >
        Within ~2 minutes / last 200m, system transitions to "arriving"
        status. This is an EVENT, not a precision increase. Courier
        remains a zone, not a pin, even during final approach.

    - scenario: Customer calls support asking "where exactly is my driver?"
      expected_behavior: >
        Support agents may access more precise internal data for
        troubleshooting, but they communicate to the customer in ETA
        and status terms, not coordinates.
```

---

```yaml
business_impact:
  financial: >
    HIGH POSITIVE. Courier retention is directly tied to unit economics —
    replacing a courier costs 3-5x their monthly contribution. Losing drivers
    to surveillance concerns is a measurable P&L hit. Meanwhile, delivery
    confidence (not GPS precision) drives customer NPS and repeat purchase
    rates. The zone-based approach preserves both revenue streams.
  operational: >
    MODERATE POSITIVE. Decoupling internal GPS (ops use) from customer-facing
    tracking (derived/approximate) creates a cleaner architecture. Internal
    systems keep full precision for routing, dispatch, and safety. Customer
    systems get a simpler, more cacheable data model.
  user: >
    POSITIVE FOR BOTH USER TYPES. Customers get what they actually need:
    confidence their delivery is coming and when. Couriers get what they
    need: dignity and the absence of being reduced to a moving dot on a
    stranger's screen. Research across the industry (DoorDash, Uber, Bolt)
    shows customers rate ETA accuracy higher than map precision in
    satisfaction surveys.
  risk: >
    MEDIUM. (1) Privacy/compliance risk: exact GPS sharing may violate GDPR
    and labor privacy regulations in EU jurisdictions — zone-based approach
    mitigates this. (2) Competitive risk: if competitors offer exact tracking,
    there may be perceived feature gap — mitigated by superior ETA accuracy
    and proactive notifications. (3) Internal political risk: Customer Success
    team may resist compromise — requires executive alignment.
  stakeholders_affected:
    - group: Customers
      impact: >
        Positive. Receive more useful information (ETA + status events)
        instead of raw data (coordinates) they don't know how to interpret.
    - group: Couriers / Drivers
      impact: >
        Strongly positive. Privacy preserved. Retention improves. Feeling
        of being treated as a professional, not a tracked asset.
    - group: Customer Success team
      impact: >
        Requires reframing. "Transparency" is preserved — customers see MORE
        useful information — but exact GPS is off the table. They need to
        understand that transparency ≠ surveillance.
    - group: Operations team
      impact: >
        Positive. Retain full internal GPS for dispatch and routing.
        Driver complaints decrease. Retention KPIs improve.
    - group: Legal / Compliance
      impact: >
        Positive. Reduced exposure to location-data privacy claims.
        Cleaner data minimization posture for GDPR/CCPA.
    - group: Product team
      impact: >
        Needs to design zone-based UX that feels premium, not degraded.
        This is a design challenge, not a limitation.
```

---

```yaml
confidence:
  level: 92
  high_confidence_areas:
    - "Exact GPS to customers is a domain violation — this is settled across the industry"
    - "ETA + status events satisfy the actual customer need for delivery confidence"
    - "Courier privacy is a genuine retention driver — not a preference, a business constraint"
    - "Zone-based approximation is the industry-standard resolution to this exact tension"
    - "Location sharing must be scoped to active delivery lifecycle"
  low_confidence_areas:
    - "Exact radius for zone approximation (200m? 500m?) — needs user research and A/B testing"
    - "Update frequency throttling (60s? 90s?) — needs performance and UX testing"
    - "Whether the specific Customer Success team will accept reframing without executive intervention"
  assumptions:
    - "Couriers are independent humans with privacy rights, not company-owned vehicles"
    - "The business operates in or may expand to jurisdictions with location-data privacy laws"
    - "Customer satisfaction is driven more by delivery predictability than location precision"
    - "Courier retention costs exceed the marginal NPS gain from exact GPS tracking"
```

---

```yaml
rejection_reasons:
  - violation: "Showing exact GPS coordinates to customers"
    business_rule_broken: >
      Violates courier privacy invariant. Violates data minimization
      principle. Creates measurable driver retention damage. Exposes the
      business to location-privacy regulatory risk. The Customer Success
      team's stated requirement encodes surveillance, not transparency.

  - violation: "Showing NO location information to customers"
    business_rule_broken: >
      Violates delivery visibility invariant. Customers have a legitimate
      business need for delivery progress confidence. Hiding all location
      context makes the experience worse than competitors and increases
      support call volume ("where is my order?"). The Operations team's
      stated requirement conflates approximate visibility with surveillance.
```

---

## Ruling

**Neither requirement as stated is acceptable. Both must be reframed.**

The correct domain model is: **Track the delivery, not the courier.**

- Expose: ETA, delivery status events, approximate zone, proactive delay notifications
- Protect: Exact GPS, courier route, courier identity beyond first name, any data outside active delivery window

This is not a compromise — it is the **domain-correct** answer. The logistics industry has converged on this pattern because it faithfully encodes what both parties actually need. Any implementation that deviates from this in either direction will be rejected.
